title: Basic Structure: Timeline
lead: The tug-of-war between Parliament and the Supreme Court — land reforms, nationalisation and privy purses on one side; Fundamental Rights and judicial review on the other.

sections.title: The Early Battles (1948–1965) — PM Nehru, Shastri

head: Milestone
What it did
rows: 1948 · Madras caste-based reservations
Madras introduced caste-based reservations in education; early 1950s: Bihar, UP, MP pass ==agrarian land reform laws== restricting ownership.
[[State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan (1951)]]
Madras 1948 law struck down — ==FR > DPSP==.

==Art 15(4)==: provisions for SEBC/SC/ST advancement (nullified Champakam) · ==Art 19(2)==: added public order, foreign relations, incitement grounds (countering [[Romesh Thappar (1950)]]) · ==Art 19(6)(ii)==: State trade monopolies · ==Art 31A==: property acquisition immune from Art 14, 19, 31 · ==Art 31B==: inserted the ==IX Schedule== — listed laws immune from FR challenge.
[[Shankari Prasad v. UoI (1951)]]
Constitutional amendments are ==not 'law' under Art 13(2)==. Two law-making powers: ==constituent== (Art 368) and ==ordinary== (Sch VII). Parliament has unlimited constituent power.
Added ==44 laws== to the IX Schedule.
[[Sajjan Singh v. State of RJ (1965)]]
Reaffirmed CA is not law under Art 13(2) (contention: 17th CA cut HC powers and needed state ratification).

sections.title: Golak Nath to Kesavananda (1967–1973) — PM I. Gandhi

head: Milestone
What it did
rows: [[Golak Nath v. State of PB (1967)]]

FRs have a ==‘transcendental’ position==; CAs ==are 'law'== under Art 13(2) (overruled Shankari Prasad, Sajjan Singh); Parliament cannot amend Part III from now on (==doctrine of prospective overruling==).
[[RC Cooper v. UoI (1970)]]
Struck down ==bank nationalisation== — inadequate compensation, violation of property rights under Art 31.
[[Madhavrao Scindia v. UoI (1971)]]
Struck down abolition of ==privy purses== (assured under Art 291, 362).

==Art 13(4)== + ==Art 368(3)==: Art 13 won't apply to CAs (negatived Golak Nath) · Art 368(1): Parliament has constituent power to amend ==any provision== · Art 368(2): President ==must assent== to CA Bills.
Art 31: inadequate compensation can't be challenged · ==Art 31C==: laws implementing ==Art 39(b)(c) > Art 14, 19, 31== and can't be challenged.
26th: removed Art 291, 362; inserted Art 363A ==abolishing privy purses== · 29th: Kerala land reform Acts added to IX Schedule.
[[Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973)]]

CA is not 'law' under Art 13 (upheld 24th CA, overruled Golak Nath) — but Parliament can amend anything ==subject to the Basic Structure== · Art 31C upheld partially: 39(b)(c) primacy stands ==BUT judicially reviewable==.

sections.title: Emergency & Aftermath (1975–1980) — PM I. Gandhi, M. Desai

head: Milestone
What it did
rows: President, VP, PM and LS Speaker elections made ==not judicially reviewable==.
[[Indira Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975)]]
Struck down the 39th CA — ==judicial review and free & fair elections are basic structure==. ==First application== of the doctrine.
==42nd CA, 1976== (the ‘mini-Constitution’)

Preamble: added ==‘socialist’, ‘secular’, integrity== · Art 31C: ==all DPSPs > Art 14, 19, 31== · curbs on Art 32, 131, 226 (undone by 43rd CA, 1977) · ==Art 368(4)==: CAs unchallengeable in court · ==Art 368(5)==: no limit on constituent power — nullifying Kesavananda; changed 54 provisions.
Removed ==property as an FR== (Art 19(1)(f), Art 31); inserted ==Art 300A== (constitutional/legal right for all persons); Art 31A–C retained; Art 31C: all DPSP > 14, 19.
[[Minerva Mills v. UoI (1980)]]
Struck down ==Art 368(4) and 368(5)== · Art 31C restored to pre-42nd position — 39(b)(c) > 14, 19 with judicial review · ==FRs and DPSPs must be read harmoniously==.

head: Milestone
What it did
rows: [[Waman Rao v. UoI (1981)]]
IX Schedule laws added ==after Kesavananda (24 April 1973)== can be challenged on basic structure; laws added before cannot (prospective overruling).
[[I.R. Coelho v. State of TN (2007)]]
Post-Kesavananda IX Schedule laws can be challenged on basic structure ==and on Arts 14, 15, 19, 21 specifically== (essential features).
[[Madras Bar Association v. UoI (2015)]]
Not just CAs — ==ordinary laws== also can't violate the basic structure.
[[SC AoR Assn. v. UoI (2015)]] (4th Judges Case)
==99th CA (NJAC) struck down== — independence of the judiciary is basic structure.
[[Anjum Qadri v. UoI (2024)]] (UP Madrassa)
Must be ==specifically shown== how an ordinary law violates the basic structure.
[[Property Owners Association v. State of MH (2024)]]
Art 31C's position per Minerva Mills and Kesavananda ==remains valid== · not all property is ==‘material resources’ under Art 39(b)== — limits government acquisition.
